one_line_takeaway: MCP's compatibility-first spec design (only 21.5% of 275 clauses are unconditional MUSTs) leaves systematic, cross-SDK implementation gaps that a language-agnostic IR + hybrid static/LLM + payload/timing modality pipeline shows are overwhelmingly (1,265/1,270) exploitable, not just benign omissions, as demonstrated on all 10 official MCP SDKs with real maintainer-acknowledged bugs including a silent tool-description prompt-injection vector in the Python SDK.
key_finding: Across the 10 SDKs, the tool found 1,588 implemented and 1,270 non-implemented clause instances (Table 2), of which 1,265 were classified as exploitable under the payload/timing modality model -- i.e. almost all non-implementations (1,265/1,270) were judged security-relevant rather than benign bugs. Per-SDK non-implementation counts ranged from 80 (Go) to 211 (PHP), and the large majority of gaps were in optional/conditional clauses rather than unconditional MUSTs (e.g. PHP missed 211 clauses, 169 of them optional, vs. Python missing 93, 76 optional). Detection accuracy (Table 3, 20 sampled cases per SDK) averaged 86% precision and 87% recall (14% average FP rate, 13.5% average FN rate), ranging from 65% precision (Python) / 76.9% recall (Swift) up to 100% precision (Swift) / 94.7% recall (Rust). Total LLM-API cost for the full 2,750 clause-SDK audit was $541.87 ($54.2/SDK, ~$0.20/clause check). Of 26 total reports submitted (3 via HackerOne, 20 to Python SDK maintainers, 2 to Go, 1 to TypeScript), 20 were acknowledged: all 3 HackerOne reports confirmed, 15/20 Python reports labeled 'ready for work' (5 of those triaged high priority: 3 P0, 2 P1), and one report each confirmed by Go and TypeScript maintainers. MCP maintainers subsequently invited the authors' tool to be integrated into the protocol's official conformance-testing Specification Enhancement Proposal (SEP). Three concrete illustrative bugs are given in the Python SDK: (1) a payload-only issue where is_token_valid() checks token presence/expiry but never verifies the token was issued by the connected server's authorization server, letting a token for one server be replayed to another; (2) a timing-only issue where MCP's SHOULD-configurable ping frequency is not actually configurable in the Python SDK, enabling unthrottled ping floods (potential DoS); (3) a payload-and-timing ('silent injection') issue where ToolListChangedNotification (and similarly ResourceListChanged/PromptListChanged notifications) are defined but never wired to the tool/resource/prompt mutation functions, so a malicious server can silently alter tool descriptions to inject instructions into the LLM's context with no client-side signal.
core_contribution: The paper identifies a new attack surface in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) called 'compatibility-abuse attacks,' which arise because the MCP spec deliberately leaves most clauses optional (only 21.5% of the 275 clauses in the 2025-06-18 spec are unconditional MUSTs, the rest are conditional MUST/SHOULD/MAY) to accommodate diverse AI agents, and official language-specific SDKs implement these optional clauses inconsistently. The authors build a three-step systematic analysis framework: (1) a Universal IR Generator that parses each of the 10 official MCP SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go, Kotlin, Swift, Java, C#, Ruby, Rust, PHP) into a language-agnostic conditional-call-graph representation (a catalog of function definitions plus the guard conditions under which they are called); (2) a hybrid static-analysis+LLM pipeline (an IR-first, self-refining loop, Algorithm 2) that uses the IR to slice code down to relevant call/definition sites and has an LLM reason over those slices (rather than the whole codebase) to judge clause compliance, falling back to a full-codebase LLM search only if confidence stays below threshold after M refinement iterations; and (3) a modality-based exploitability analysis that classifies each non-implemented clause along two axes -- attacker control of message payload and attacker control of message timing -- yielding four modalities (both-controllable PyTy, payload-only PyTn, timing-only PnTy, non-exploitable PnTn), and treats only the first three as security-relevant.
threat_model: A malicious or compromised MCP server; the MCP client and backend LLM are assumed to be under the control of (and trusted by) the victim user. The authors justify this asymmetry because public third-party MCP servers vary in quality/trustworthiness while clients are typically shipped by LLM vendors as part of official platform integrations, and because existing MCP vulnerability detectors adopt the same assumption. The paper explicitly notes the exploit direction is bidirectional in principle (client-side clause omissions can also be attacked), but the primary motivating example and worked cases in the paper use the malicious-server direction.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the IR-based analyzer is a deliberate universal/cross-SDK design trade-off that favors scalability over per-language precision, so it can over-approximate and may miss complex, language-specific clause implementations (contributing to the observed FP/FN rates, e.g. Python's 35% FP rate and Swift's 30% FN rate in the sampled evaluation). The MCP spec itself is a fast-moving target (five substantive spec versions in about one year, plus new SDKs like Ruby onboarded mid-study), which stresses the tool's maintainability. The authors chose not to individually report all 1,265 potential risks due to reporting/triage bandwidth (~30 min per report to prepare, ~2 days average maintainer triage time), instead pushing for SEP tool integration -- meaning the vast majority of flagged issues are unverified by any human beyond the automated pipeline itself; only a small hand-picked seed set of Python SDK cases is discussed in detail, and exploitation mechanics for all cases are intentionally redacted from the paper for responsible disclosure, so the paper's own text does not let a reader independently verify end-to-end exploitability of the 1,265 count. The threat model is asymmetric (malicious-server-only in the worked examples) even though the authors note client-side omissions are symmetrically exploitable but do not analyze that direction in detail.

datasets_used: All 10 official MCP SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go, Kotlin, Swift, Java, C#, Ruby, Rust, PHP) as source-code corpora
MCP protocol specification, 2025-06-08/2025-06-18 stable version, 275 total clauses
quantitative_results: 275 total MCP spec clauses: 59 (21.5%) unconditional MUST, 78 (28.4%) conditional MUST, 138 (50.2%) non-MUST (SHOULD/MAY)

Across 10 SDKs (Table 2 totals): 1,588 implemented clause instances, 1,015 non-implementations of optional clauses (NI-O), 275 non-implementations of non-optional clauses (NI-NO), 1,270 total non-implementations, 1,265 classified exploitable
Per-SDK non-implementation totals (Table 2): Python 93, TypeScript 82, Go 80, Kotlin 147, Swift 170, Java 106, C# 98, Ruby 167, Rust 116, PHP 211

Detection accuracy (Table 3, 20 sampled cases/SDK): average 86% precision, 87% recall; 14% average FP rate, 13.5% average FN rate; per-SDK precision range 65% (Python) to 100% (Swift); recall range 76.9% (Swift) to 94.7% (Rust)
Cost: $541.87 total to audit 275 clauses x 10 SDKs (2,750 checks); ~$54.2/SDK; ~$0.20/clause check; estimated ~$0.60 per confirmed true-positive violation

Disclosure outcomes: 26 reports submitted total (3 HackerOne, 20 Python SDK, 2 Go, 1 TypeScript); 20 acknowledged overall; all 3 HackerOne reports confirmed; 15/20 Python reports labeled ready-for-work, 5 of those triaged high priority (3 P0, 2 P1); 1 Go and 1 TypeScript report each confirmed
baselines_compared: No quantitative head-to-head baseline comparison is run; the paper qualitatively contrasts itself against existing template/pattern-based MCP scanners (mcp-scan/mcpscan.ai, Akto, Palo Alto Networks Prisma AIRS, DataDome, MCPGuardian, Prompt Security) on scope grounds -- these tools target server-level runtime scanning with fixed attack templates (e.g. mcp-scan detects only prompt injection, tool poisoning, and toxic flows) and do not analyze SDK-level clause implementation gaps, unlike this paper's SDK/clause-centric approach
